$comment: x86_64 target descriptor (plan 12 §2.6 ML5 cycle 2 pivot — config-driven targets). Parallels src/dss-config/sources/*.lang.json: a compile target is a config file the LIR substrate reads, NOT C++ code. Adding a new target = drop a new .target.json. Loaded via TargetSchema::loadShipped("x86_64").

target.name: x86_64
target.description: x86_64 opcode set + physical register file + SysV AMD64 / Microsoft x64 calling conventions. Cycle 2b: regfile/ABI added. Cycle 3 (instruction selection) expands the opcode set to full c-subset corpus coverage.
target.$reservedFields: The per-opcode minOperands/maxOperands fields are documented in the JSON for completeness but are NOT yet fully enforced by the substrate. Terminator-ness derives from `terminatorKind` (Br/CondBr/Switch/Return/Unreachable; default `none` = non-terminator); the parser's `parseInst` and `LirVerifier` consult it directly. Per-kind successor-count is enforced by `TargetSchemaData::validate()` via the shared `kTargetTerminatorShapes` table.

opcodes.$comment: Slot 0 = the Invalid sentinel. Every target must declare it as the first opcode so the substrate's `opcode == 0` check (in LirBuilder::addInst) catches default-constructed insts loudly.

Virtual-ISA pseudo-op: 'this LirReg is the function's k-th input parameter' (payload = k). ML7 calling-convention lowering rewrites `arg` to the real mov-from-arg-register sequence; before that, it represents the parameter as a virtreg that ML5 cycle 3 MIR→LIR isel can consume directly. Every target backend needs SOME representation of 'i-th function parameter'; pre-regalloc this is the uniform shape.

2-operand-shape mov: destination = result vreg, source = operands[0]. AS2 cycle 2 wires this to two encoding variants: REX.W 0x8B /r (mov r64, r64) when source is a register; REX.W 0xC7 /0 imm32 (mov r/m64, imm32 sign-extended to 64-bit) when source is an immediate.

add reg-reg — REX.W 0x01 /r (ADD r/m64, r64). LIR is 3-address (dst, src1, src2); requires2Address=true makes the legalize pass insert `mov dst, src1` first when dst!=src1, then the assembler emits `add dst, src2` (writing into ModR/M.rm, which is dst).

encoding.$comment: 2-address shape: `operands[0]` IS the destination after `lir_2addr_legalize` ensures result==operands[0]. So no resultSlot — the wire for operands[0] handles the destination placement (its hwEncoding goes into ModR/M.rm). validate() rule G has a `requires2Address` exception for exactly this shape.

variants.$comment: add reg-imm32 — REX.W 0x81 /0 imm32 (ADD r/m64, imm32). Used by ML7 epilogue's `add rsp, N` stack-frame teardown. 2-address shape: operands[0] is dest, imm32 is immediate. modrmRegExt=0 fills ModR/M.reg with the opcode-extension digit (/0 for ADD).

opcodes.$comment: sub reg-reg — REX.W 0x29 /r (SUB r/m64, r64). Same 2-address shape as `add`.

variants.$comment: sub reg-imm32 — REX.W 0x81 /5 imm32 (SUB r/m64, imm32). Used by ML7 prologue's `sub rsp, N` stack-frame setup. 2-address shape (operands[0] is dest, imm32 is immediate). modrmRegExt=5 fills ModR/M.reg with the opcode-extension digit (/5 for SUB).

opcodes.$comment: 2-operand multiply. x86 has both 2-op `imul reg, reg` (writes the low half to the destination — what we emit) and 1-op `mul/imul reg` (rdx:rax full-width — needed only for overflow/128-bit results, future cycle). The 2-op form is REX.W 0x0F 0xAF /r — UNUSUAL among x86 binary arith because dest goes to ModR/M.reg (not r/m), source-1 goes to r/m. Wires reflect this: op[0]→modrm.reg (the destination per 2-address), op[1]→modrm.rm.

Materialize a 0/1 boolean from FLAGS. Payload carries the TargetCondCode (eq/ne/slt/.../uge); the LIR substrate is condition-blind. ML6 regalloc treats FLAGS as an implicit predecessor; AS1 emits the real x86_64 setcc-byte + movzx zero-extend sequence.

Hard-trap / 'this code path is unreachable'. AS1 maps to x86_64 ud2 (encoding `0F 0B`). Encoding added at D-LK10-ENTRY Slice A (2026-06-02) so the trampoline emitter can append `unreachable` after noreturn syscall / call_indirect_via_extern call sites — reaches `assemble()` and must emit bytes (the `TargetEncodingShape::None` arm in `asm.cpp` fails loud `A_NoEncodingDeclared`). Shape mirrors the `ret` opcode (zero-operand variant, no ModR/M, opcode bytes literal).

D-LK10-ENTRY Slice A: raw kernel transition (Linux exit_group / Mach-O BSD exit). Zero-operand, no result, no successors, NOT a terminator (general-case opcode: FF6 hello-world's `write` syscall returns to the next instruction; only the trampoline's `exit` syscall is noreturn, and that's signaled by the trampoline emitter appending a separate `unreachable` after). Encoding: x86_64 SYSCALL `0F 05` — fixed 2-byte opcode, same shape vocabulary as `ret` and the new `unreachable`. The syscall-number register is loaded into `rax` (or per the target's `syscallNumGpr` field on format schema's `processExit` — Slice B) by a preceding LIR `mov` op, NOT a payload on `syscall` itself.

D-LK10-ENTRY Slice A: indirect call through an extern-import IAT slot (Windows kernel32!ExitProcess, future macOS libSystem imports). x86_64 encoding `FF 15 disp32` (call qword ptr [RIP + disp32]) — the same 4-byte disp32 + REL32 reloc shape as the existing direct `call` opcode (E8 disp32), just with a 2-byte opcode literal `[255, 21]` (FF + ModR/M byte 15h = mod=00 reg=/2 rm=101 → [RIP+disp32]). NOT a terminator (general-case indirect call; the trampoline emitter appends `unreachable` after when the callee is noreturn like ExitProcess). 1 operand = ExternImport SymbolRef. The `rel32` reloc-kind row (in this file's `relocations[]` array) computes `S - P - 4` which is exactly what `[RIP+disp32]` resolution needs at load time (S = IAT slot VA, P = the disp32 patch site, RIP at execution = patch + 4).

call sym — direct near call to a symbol. Encoding: 0xE8 + 4-byte signed PC-relative displacement to the call target (filled with 0 by the assembler; the linker patches via the rel32 relocation). The schema's maxOperands=255 admits a call ABI where extra operands carry arg-register hints; cycle-4 wires the single-SymbolRef variant (the direct-call form). An indirect-call form (call rax, encoding REX 0xFF /2) is anchored as a future variant.

ret — 0xC3 near-return. The schema's maxOperands=1 admits an optional return value carrier; AS2 cycle 2 wires the zero-operand variant only. The actual return value is materialized into the calling-convention's return register by ML7 callconv-lowering BEFORE the ret instruction is emitted (via a `mov` to rax/xmm0); the ret instruction itself never carries an operand at the byte level.

variants.$comment: ret — no operands at the LIR level. ML7 cycle 2's callee-side return-value materialization moves the MIR Return's vreg into cc.returnGprs[0]/returnFprs[0] BEFORE the ret, so the assembler sees a 0-operand ret form.

opcodes.$comment: Allocate a stack-frame slot (cycle 3c). Cycle 3c is the pass-through layer: payload is threaded verbatim from MIR Alloca (currently 0 — MIR does not yet encode size/align; that lands when ML6's frame-layout phase pulls on it). Result is a pointer-class virtreg holding the slot's effective address. AS1 turns this into a frame-relative `lea` once the frame layout is known.

Load from memory. Operands: [base_reg, MemBase(scale=1), MemOffset(disp)]. Optional index-reg arm added in cycle 3d. Emits `mov reg, [base + disp]`: REX.W 0x8B /r ModR/M(mod=10, reg=result, rm=base) [SIB 0x24 when base.lo3==4] disp32. Plan 13 §3.1 D-AS4-1 — landed alongside ML7 cycle 2 to close D-LK10-2 byte-on-disk path.

variants.$comment: 4-op indexed form (D-AS4-5): `mov reg, [base + index*scale + disp32]`. The walker emits SIB unconditionally when SibIndex wires.

opcodes.$comment: Store to memory. Operands: [value_reg, base_reg, MemBase(scale), MemOffset(disp)] OR [value_reg, base_reg, index_reg, MemBase(scale), MemOffset(disp)]. No result. Emits `mov [base (+ index*scale) + disp32], reg`: REX.W 0x89 /r ModR/M(mod=10, reg=value, rm=base) [SIB] disp32. Plan 13 §3.1 D-AS4-1 + D-AS4-5.

Load-effective-address. Operands: [base_reg, MemBase(scale), MemOffset(disp)] OR [base_reg, index_reg, MemBase(scale), MemOffset(disp)]. AS1 emits x86_64 `lea reg, [base + index*scale + disp]`. Cycle 3c uses the 3-operand form for MIR Gep without an index; index-arm via 4-operand form. Encoding (D-AS4-5 close 2026-06-01): REX.W 0x8D /r ModR/M(mod=10, reg=result, rm=base) [SIB] disp32. Opcode 0x8D is the `lea` mnemonic; the rest matches the `load`/`store` shape since lea reads memory operands but writes only the EA (no memory access).
Sign-extend integer (cycle 3c cast lowering for MIR SExt). Operands: [src_reg]. Width comes from the result reg's class + the source reg's width-of-record. AS1 emits movsx.
Zero-extend integer (cycle 3c cast lowering for MIR ZExt). Operands: [src_reg]. AS1 emits movzx.
Truncate integer (cycle 3c cast lowering for MIR Trunc). Operands: [src_reg]. AS1 emits mov of the low N bits (effectively a no-op on x86_64 since registers alias subregs).
Bitwise AND. Cycle 3d bitwise wave — same shape as add/sub/mul. AS1 emits x86_64 `and`.
Logical left shift. Same opcode shape as add/sub.
Logical right shift (LShr). AS1 emits x86_64 `shr`.
Arithmetic right shift (AShr). AS1 emits x86_64 `sar`.
Bitwise NOT — 1-operand. AS1 emits x86_64 `not`.
Integer two's-complement negate. AS1 emits x86_64 `neg`.
Float arithmetic (cycle 3d FPR wave). FAdd → `addsd`/`addss` per width; cycle 3d emits the abstract opcode and lets AS1 pick the right scalar variant from the operand width.
Float unary negate. AS1 emits xorps reg, [.LC0_signbit] OR subss zero, reg — the abstract opcode hides this.
Float-to-float precision change (FPTrunc f64→f32, FPExt f32→f64). AS1 emits cvtsd2ss / cvtss2sd.
Float-to-signed-integer conversion (FPToSI). AS1 emits cvttss2si / cvttsd2si (truncating).
Float-to-unsigned-integer conversion (FPToUI). x86 has no direct opcode; AS1 emits a guarded cvttsd2si sequence.
Signed-integer-to-float (SIToFP). AS1 emits cvtsi2ss / cvtsi2sd.
Unsigned-integer-to-float (UIToFP). Similar AS1 guarded sequence.

Cross-class bit-pattern move (GPR↔FPR). AS1 emits x86_64 `movq` (the QWord variant). Cycle-3d Bitcast lowers via this opcode whenever src and dst register classes differ; same-class Bitcasts continue to use `mov` (handled by cycle 3c).

Intrinsic call. Payload = intrinsic id (per HirIntrinsicRegistry / MIR convention). Operands = args. Result is optional (some intrinsics produce values, some don't). AS1 maps the intrinsic id to its concrete sequence (e.g. memcpy, debugtrap, sqrt) per target. Cycle 3e lowers MIR IntrinsicCall to this opcode.

Spill reload pseudo-op. Payload = spill-slot index (LirSpillSlot.v). Result = the reloaded value's physical reg. The encoder/ML7 materializes this as `mov result, [stackPointer + slotOffset]`; the exact stackPointer + offset come from per-target callconv lowering. Target-blind at LIR tier.

Spill store pseudo-op. Payload = spill-slot index. Operand[0] = the physical reg whose value is being stored. The encoder/ML7 materializes this as `mov [stackPointer + slotOffset], operand`. Target-blind at LIR tier.

$registersComment: Physical register file. Ordinal = position in this array (consumed by `LirReg::id` post-regalloc). Only the registers ML6 regalloc + ML7 callconv reference are declared here — sub-byte aliases (al/ah/bl/...) are omitted until a real consumer needs them. Width is in bytes (8/4/2/1). hwEncoding = ModR/M ordinal the assembler/encoder will read.
$callingConventionsComment: Per-platform x86_64 ABIs. ML7 callconv lowering selects one based on driver flag / function attribute. SysV AMD64 = Linux/macOS/BSD; ms_x64 = Windows. argGprs/argFprs ordering is significant (caller MUST place int args in those registers in that order, then spill to stack).

callingConventions.name: sysv_amd64
callingConventions.$comment: System V AMD64 ABI (Linux, macOS, BSD). Integer args: RDI/RSI/RDX/RCX/R8/R9 then stack. Float args: XMM0..7 then stack. Integer return: RAX (low 64 bits) + RDX (high 64 bits if >64-bit aggregate). Float return: XMM0/XMM1. Stack 16-byte aligned BEFORE call (RSP%16==0); the call instruction pushes RIP, leaving RSP%16==8 at callee entry. 128-byte red zone below RSP for leaf functions.
callingConventions.$entryStackPointerBiasComment: D-LK10-ENTRY-TRAMP-PROLOGUE: at SysV process entry the kernel maps the image and JUMPS to `_start` with RSP 16-byte-aligned and NO return address pushed — so RSP bias mod stackAlignment is 0. The trampoline emitter computes its prologue `sub rsp, N` via `alignedSizeWithBias(shadowSpaceBytes=0, stackAlignment=16, bias=0) = 0` — no prologue emitted, which is correct for SysV (no shadow space + already aligned).

callingConventions.name: ms_x64
callingConventions.$comment: Microsoft x64 ABI (Windows). First 4 args in RCX/RDX/R8/R9 regardless of int-vs-float (slot-aligned: an XMM arg in slot N uses XMM_N AND consumes the matching GPR slot, even if the GPR is unused; symmetrically, an integer in slot N consumes XMM_N). Integer return: RAX. Float return: XMM0. Stack 16-byte aligned before call; 32-byte shadow space (home space) always allocated by caller for callee to spill arg registers. No red zone.
callingConventions.$entryStackPointerBiasComment: D-LK10-ENTRY-TRAMP-PROLOGUE: at Windows process entry, `RtlUserThreadStart` does a CALL into the entry point, so RSP at the first trampoline instruction is ≡8 mod 16. The trampoline emitter computes its prologue `sub rsp, N` via `alignedSizeWithBias(shadowSpaceBytes=32, stackAlignment=16, bias=8)`: smallest N >= 32 with N ≡ 8 mod 16 = 40. That single `sub rsp, 0x28` (a) reserves 32 bytes of caller-side shadow space for ExitProcess + (b) realigns RSP from ≡8 to ≡0 mod 16 so both `call user_entry` and `call [iat_ExitProcess]` enter their callees at the ABI-mandated RSP ≡8 mod 16. Without this field wired, ExitProcess's aligned-SSE stores produced STATUS_ACCESS_VIOLATION on CLI-emitted binaries (smoke test `mov eax,42; ret` hid it because the test fn never touched the stack and the specific ExitProcess code path didn't hit movaps with misaligned RSP).

$relocationsComment: Relocation taxonomy (plan 13 AS4 + plan 14 LK6 cycle 1 + D-LK6-1 closure 2026-06-01). Opaque uint32 `kind` tags the assembler stamps onto each emitted Relocation; the linker reads via TargetSchema::relocationInfo(kind). `formula` is a CLOSED-ENUM DISCRIMINATOR (load-bearing — see RelocFormulaKind in target_schema.hpp): 'linear' (S + A + (pcRel ? -P : 0) + addendBias; widthBytes LE), or one of the bit-shift variants ('aarch64_call26' / 'aarch64_adr_prel_pg_hi21' / 'aarch64_add_abs_lo12'). For 'linear', the structured fields (pcRelative + addendBias + widthBytes) parameterize the formula. Tag values are arbitrary per-target — only the NAME round-trips with format.json cross-reference.
